content:
The **regression vs classification** split predates modern machine learning. **19th-century statistics** treated **linear regression** on continuous measurements (Galton, Pearson) separately from **discriminant analysis** and **logistic models** for labeled groups.

**Fisher's linear discriminant** (1936) formalized separating discrete classes with a linear boundary — the statistical ancestor of linear classifiers.

**Logistic regression** grew from **logit models** in biostatistics (1950s–60s) to estimate **odds of a binary outcome**, cementing the naming confusion: "regression" in the method name, **classification** in the task.

Computer **pattern recognition** (1960s–80s) reused the same labeled (x, y) framework for both numeric prediction and category recognition.

**Neural networks** unified the machinery: shared hidden layers with task-specific output heads — linear for regression, **softmax** for multi-class — by the 1990s.

Today's **deep learning** and **LLM fine-tuning** still branch on target type: regression heads for reward or score modeling, classification heads for safety, routing, and intent labels.

timeline.label: Linear regression on measurements (1800s–1900s)
timeline.description: Statisticians model continuous outcomes — early supervised regression on numeric y.

timeline.label: Fisher's discriminant (1936)
timeline.description: Linear separation of labeled groups — roots of supervised classification.

timeline.label: Logistic / logit models (mid-1900s)
timeline.description: Binary outcome modeling via log-odds; name "regression" persists for classification tasks.

timeline.label: Pattern recognition era (1960s–1980s)
timeline.description: Perceptrons and statistical classifiers train on labeled feature vectors for discrete decisions.

timeline.label: Unified neural heads (1990s–present)
timeline.description: Shared representations with linear or softmax outputs; same pipeline, different target types.
